body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

Uh, so what what changed, uh, between last time and this time.
So a big change is that we decided to strongly associate datagrams with the HTTP request that created or that is associated with them.

So, um, what that means is we kind of removed the flow ID, which was the a single, uh, variant at the start of the datagram for Demultiplexing, and we replace it by the stream ID of the associated request.

And since an HTTP three, um requests are on client initiated bidirectional streams, which in quick v1 are, uh, divisible by four, we just encode a quarter of that because that means we can save some bits, uh, in the variant encoding, uh, and these datagrams can also carry a context ID, which is optional.

And the idea there is for applications, uh, that need it, you can have a second multiplexing layer with some, uh, negotiation or declaration capabilities that allows you, for example, if you have an IP tunnel on one context and you want to compress, uh, the IP header, the IP addresses and some of them.
These can coexist on the same conceptual request and on the same.
So datagrams with the same stream ID field.
Um, and we're gonna go into more detail about how you communicate these encodings to your peers.
So they know the format of the datagrams you're sending.
Um, so, uh, another thing is, since we've agreed as a working group that we need to support versions of HTTP that, uh, before three so that go over TCP, we need a way to send datagrams.
And in this case of UDP, UDP packets over streams.
Uh, and so that was originally done using something called stream chunks, which were a sequence of tlvs, uh, on the data stream.
So what I'm calling the data stream is the in uh, in HTTP two and three.
It's what's in data frames.
Uh, if you want.
It was like the only thing that was there.
Uh, some, uh, details there.
But anyway, um, and the problem there is the original design.
We were just thinking, well, we just can just put everything in data, and that way the intermediaries don't need to think about it.

Uh, and so the problem is what happens in the scenario that we have a diagram here for where the intermediary is speaking HTTP three to the client on one side, http2 to the server on the other, um, and a and some stream chunks are flowing.
And then a datagram frame comes in.
The intermediary needs to kind of inject that kind of between two tlvs.
So it needs to pick a moment in the stream to inject it.
But in order to do that, it means that it needs to be parsing the entire stream of tlvs.
And that kind of completely breaks down the architectural purity we had of, okay, the data stream is something that intermediaries don't touch.
So because of this, we decided to switch to a new approach, um, which is capsules.
Um, thanks Ben for the name.
Even though I know Ben's not too excited about the name.
Uh, and so the idea there is, if the data stream isn't great, how do we get information, uh, end to end between the client and the server in the presence of intermediaries?
Because you can't just use Http2 or GP3 frames because these are hop by hop.
If a frame arrives at an intermediary, it only has meaning from there.
The interpreter is not going to blindly take it and forward it on the other side, especially if it's a frame that it doesn't know, the spec says you just drop it on the floor.
Uh, and so what's a capsule?

It's just like it is a new type of frame, but it has a new semantic, which is when an intermediary receives a capsule, it even if it doesn't know what this capsule is, it copies it and forwards it onto the stream that it would forward the data on.
And that gives you kind of a new extension point to HTTP um that allows defining new capsules, but you don't need to change the intermediary every time.
You only modify the intermediary once.
Um, and then, uh, the way we'll define this for H2 is kind of probably use upgrade and define a new protocol that is a sequence of capsules.
But we'll cross that bridge when we get there.
